title: Fibonacci heap
description: Implement priority queue using Fibonacci heap

examples.input: Initialize pq, insert 5, 2, 8, 1, extract-min, extract-min, insert 3, extract-min
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize empty array []. Step 2: Insert 5 -> array becomes [5]. Step 3: Insert 2 -> array becomes [5, 2]. Step 4: Insert 8 -> array becomes [5, 2, 8]. Step 5: Insert 1 -> array becomes [5, 2, 8, 1]. Step 6: Extract-min: scan array [5,2,8,1], find min at index 3 (value 1). Swap arr[3] with arr[-1]: array becomes [5,2,8,1] -> after swap [5,2,1,8]. Pop last: array becomes [5,2,1], return 1. Step 7: Extract-min: scan [5,2,1], find min at index 2 (value 1). Swap arr[2] with arr[-1]: [5,2,1] -> [5,1,2]. Pop last: array becomes [5,1], return 2. Step 8: Insert 3 -> array becomes [5,1,3]. Step 9: Extract-min: scan [5,1,3], find min at index 1 (value 1). Swap arr[1] with arr[-1]: [5,1,3] -> [5,3,1]. Pop last: array becomes [5,3], return 1.

examples.input: Initialize pq, insert 10, extract-min, insert 7, 4, extract-min, extract-min, extract-min
examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize empty array []. Step 2: Insert 10 -> array becomes [10]. Step 3: Extract-min: scan [10], min at index 0. Swap arr[0] with arr[-1]: [10] -> [10] (same). Pop: array becomes [], return 10. Step 4: Insert 7 -> array becomes [7]. Step 5: Insert 4 -> array becomes [7,4]. Step 6: Extract-min: scan [7,4], min at index 1 (value 4). Swap arr[1] with arr[-1]: [7,4] -> [4,7]. Pop: array becomes [4], return 4. Step 7: Extract-min: scan [4], min at index 0. Swap arr[0] with arr[-1]: [4] -> [4]. Pop: array becomes [], return 7. Step 8: Extract-min: array empty, return None.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Naive Array-Based Priority Queue
approaches.summary: Use a simple array to store elements and sort or scan for each operation, such as inserting by appending and extracting by finding the minimum via linear scan. This is straightforward but inefficient for large datasets.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) for insert and extract-min, O(1) for other operations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Priority Queue Using Naive Array-Based Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a priority queue using a simple array with linear scan operations for insert and extract-min.
ladders.explanation: This approach implements a priority queue using a basic array data structure. The intuition is straightforward: we maintain an unordered list of elements and perform operations by scanning the array when needed. For insertion, we simply append the new element to the end of the array (O(1) amortized). For extract-min, we scan through the entire array to find the minimum element, remove it, and return it (O(n)). Other operations like peek (view minimum without removal) also require linear scanning. This works because we're trading simplicity for efficiency - we avoid complex data structures but pay with linear time operations. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty array. 2) Insert: append element to array. 3) Extract-min: find minimum via linear scan, remove it by swapping with last element and popping, return minimum. 4) Peek: find minimum via linear scan without removal. 5) Size: return array length. Time complexity: O(1) for insert (amortized), O(n) for extract-min and peek, O(1) for size. Space: O(n). Connection to parent: This is the complete implementation of the naive approach. Common mistakes include: not handling empty queue cases, inefficient removal (shifting entire array instead of swap-and-pop), forgetting to update size after removal. Real code pattern: class PriorityQueue: def __init__(self): self.arr = []; def insert(self, val): self.arr.append(val); def extract_min(self): if not self.arr: return None; min_idx = 0; for i in range(1, len(self.arr)): if self.arr[i] < self.arr[min_idx]: min_idx = i; min_val = self.arr[min_idx]; self.arr[min_idx] = self.arr[-1]; self.arr.pop(); return min_val.

examples.input: Initialize pq, insert 5, 2, 8, 1, extract-min, extract-min, insert 3, extract-min
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty array []. Step 2: Insert 5 -> array becomes [5]. Step 3: Insert 2 -> array becomes [5, 2]. Step 4: Insert 8 -> array becomes [5, 2, 8]. Step 5: Insert 1 -> array becomes [5, 2, 8, 1]. Step 6: Extract-min: scan array [5,2,8,1], find min at index 3 (value 1). Swap arr[3] with arr[-1]: array becomes [5,2,8,1] -> after swap [5,2,1,8]. Pop last: array becomes [5,2,1], return 1. Step 7: Extract-min: scan [5,2,1], find min at index 2 (value 1). Swap arr[2] with arr[-1]: [5,2,1] -> [5,1,2]. Pop last: array becomes [5,1], return 2. Step 8: Insert 3 -> array becomes [5,1,3]. Step 9: Extract-min: scan [5,1,3], find min at index 1 (value 1). Swap arr[1] with arr[-1]: [5,1,3] -> [5,3,1]. Pop last: array becomes [5,3], return 1.

examples.input: Initialize pq, insert 10, extract-min, insert 7, 4, extract-min, extract-min, extract-min
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty array []. Step 2: Insert 10 -> array becomes [10]. Step 3: Extract-min: scan [10], min at index 0. Swap arr[0] with arr[-1]: [10] -> [10] (same). Pop: array becomes [], return 10. Step 4: Insert 7 -> array becomes [7]. Step 5: Insert 4 -> array becomes [7,4]. Step 6: Extract-min: scan [7,4], min at index 1 (value 4). Swap arr[1] with arr[-1]: [7,4] -> [4,7]. Pop: array becomes [4], return 4. Step 7: Extract-min: scan [4], min at index 0. Swap arr[0] with arr[-1]: [4] -> [4]. Pop: array becomes [], return 7. Step 8: Extract-min: array empty, return None.

testCases.input: Initialize, insert 100, insert 50, extract-min, insert 75, extract-min, extract-min
Initialize, insert 1, insert 2, insert 3, extract-min, insert 0, extract-min, extract-min
Initialize, insert 6, 2, 8, 1, 9, extract-min, insert 0, extract-min, extract-min

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan to Find and Remove Minimum Element
ladders.desc: Implement the core operation of scanning an array to find the minimum element and remove it efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the naive priority queue's extract-min operation. The intuition is that to find the minimum in an unordered collection, we must examine every element. We maintain a running minimum as we traverse the array. Once found, we need to remove it efficiently without shifting all elements (which would be O(n) additional time). The clever optimization is to swap the minimum element with the last element, then pop from the end - this gives O(1) removal time. Step-by-step: 1) Handle empty array case (return None or throw error). 2) Initialize min_index = 0, min_value = arr[0]. 3) Traverse array from index 1 to end: if arr[i] < min_value, update min_index and min_value. 4) After scan, swap arr[min_index] with arr[-1]. 5) Pop last element (which now contains the minimum). 6) Return min_value. Time: O(n) for scan, O(1) for swap/pop. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This is the extract_min method in the full implementation. Common mistakes: forgetting empty check, not updating min_value when updating min_index, inefficient removal by shifting elements. Real code pattern: def extract_min(arr): if not arr: return None; min_idx = 0; for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] < arr[min_idx]: min_idx = i; min_val = arr[min_idx]; arr[min_idx] = arr[-1]; arr.pop(); return min_val.

examples.input: Array: [5, 2, 8, 1], perform extract-min operation
examples.trace: Step 1: Array not empty, proceed. Step 2: Initialize min_idx = 0, min_val = arr[0] = 5. Step 3: i=1: arr[1]=2 < 5, so min_idx=1, min_val=2. Step 4: i=2: arr[2]=8 > 2, no change. Step 5: i=3: arr[3]=1 < 2, so min_idx=3, min_val=1. Step 6: Scan complete, min_idx=3, min_val=1. Step 7: Swap arr[3] with arr[-1]: [5,2,8,1] -> swap positions 3 and 3 (same), array still [5,2,8,1]. Step 8: Pop last: remove 1, array becomes [5,2,8]. Step 9: Return 1.

examples.input: Array: [10], perform extract-min operation
examples.trace: Step 1: Array not empty, proceed. Step 2: Initialize min_idx = 0, min_val = arr[0] = 10. Step 3: No traversal needed (len=1). Step 4: Swap arr[0] with arr[-1]: [10] -> swap position 0 with 0, array still [10]. Step 5: Pop last: remove 10, array becomes []. Step 6: Return 10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Minimum in Unordered Array with Swap-and-Pop Removal
ladders.desc: Understand the technique of finding minimum via linear scan and removing it efficiently by swapping with last element.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in an unordered array, finding the minimum requires examining all elements (O(n)), but removal can be optimized to O(1) using a swap-and-pop technique. The intuition: arrays have O(1) access to any index and O(1) amortized append/pop from end, but removing from middle requires shifting. By swapping the target element with the last element, we move it to a position where removal is cheap. This works because order doesn't matter in our naive priority queue - we only care about finding minima, not maintaining sorted order. Step-by-step: 1) Linear scan to find index of minimum. 2) Store minimum value. 3) Swap element at min_index with last element. 4) Remove last element (now contains the minimum). 5) Return stored minimum. Time: O(n) for scan, O(1) for swap/pop. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the algorithm behind extract_min. Common mistakes: swapping before finding minimum (corrupts array), not handling single-element case correctly, forgetting that swap with self is harmless but should be considered. Real code pattern: min_idx = 0; for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i] < arr[min_idx]: min_idx = i; min_val = arr[min_idx]; arr[min_idx], arr[-1] = arr[-1], arr[min_idx]; arr.pop(); return min_val.

examples.input: Find and remove minimum from [4, 2, 7, 1, 3]
examples.trace: Step 1: Find min: start min_idx=0 (value 4). Compare with index 1: 2 < 4, update min_idx=1. Index 2: 7 > 2, no change. Index 3: 1 < 2, update min_idx=3. Index 4: 3 > 1, no change. Min at index 3, value 1. Step 2: Swap arr[3] with arr[-1]: [4,2,7,1,3] -> swap positions 3 and 4: becomes [4,2,7,3,1]. Step 3: Pop last: remove 1, array becomes [4,2,7,3]. Step 4: Return 1.

examples.input: Find and remove minimum from [5, 5, 5]
examples.trace: Step 1: Find min: start min_idx=0 (value 5). Compare with index 1: 5 == 5, no change. Index 2: 5 == 5, no change. Min at index 0, value 5. Step 2: Swap arr[0] with arr[-1]: [5,5,5] -> swap positions 0 and 2: becomes [5,5,5] (same values). Step 3: Pop last: remove 5, array becomes [5,5]. Step 4: Return 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Linear Search Through Array to Find Minimum Element
ladders.desc: Implement algorithm to scan through an array and identify the smallest element.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental operation of finding the minimum value in an unordered array using linear search. The intuition is simple: to determine the smallest element, you must examine every element because without any ordering, any element could be the minimum. We maintain a 'current minimum' as we traverse, updating it whenever we find a smaller element. Step-by-step: 1) If array empty, return None or handle error. 2) Initialize min_value = first element, min_index = 0. 3) For each subsequent element (index 1 to n-1): compare with current min_value. If smaller, update min_value and min_index. 4) After loop, return min_value and/or min_index. Time: O(n) since we examine each element once. Space: O(1) for variables. Connection to L2: This is the first half of the extract-min operation (before removal). Common mistakes: starting comparison from index 0 instead of 1 (wasteful but correct), forgetting empty case, not tracking index (needed for removal). Real code pattern: def find_min(arr): if not arr: return None; min_val = arr[0]; min_idx = 0; for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] < min_val: min_val = arr[i]; min_idx = i; return min_val, min_idx.

examples.input: Find minimum in array: [5, 2, 8, 1]
examples.trace: Step 1: Array not empty. Step 2: Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 5, min_idx = 0. Step 3: i=1: arr[1]=2, 2 < 5, so update min_val=2, min_idx=1. Step 4: i=2: arr[2]=8, 8 > 2, no update. Step 5: i=3: arr[3]=1, 1 < 2, so update min_val=1, min_idx=3. Step 6: Return min_val=1, min_idx=3.

examples.input: Find minimum in array: [10]
examples.trace: Step 1: Array not empty. Step 2: Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 10, min_idx = 0. Step 3: No loop (len=1). Step 4: Return min_val=10, min_idx=0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swap Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Implement the operation of exchanging values at two indices in an array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves swapping the values at two specified indices in an array. The intuition is that to exchange two values, we need a temporary variable to hold one value during the swap to avoid overwriting. This is a fundamental array manipulation technique. Step-by-step: 1) Check indices are valid (within array bounds). 2) Store value at first index in temp variable. 3) Assign value at second index to first index. 4) Assign temp value to second index. Time: O(1). Space: O(1) for temp variable. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the removal step after finding minimum (swap min element with last element). Common mistakes: swapping without temp (loses data), not validating indices, assuming indices are different (swapping same index is harmless). Real code pattern: def swap(arr, i, j): if 0 <= i < len(arr) and 0 <= j < len(arr): temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; else: handle error.

examples.input: Array: [5, 2, 8, 1], swap indices 1 and 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Check indices: i=1, j=3, both valid (0-3). Step 2: temp = arr[1] = 2. Step 3: arr[1] = arr[3] = 1. Step 4: arr[3] = temp = 2. Result: [5, 1, 8, 2].

examples.input: Array: [10], swap indices 0 and 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Check indices: i=0, j=0, both valid. Step 2: temp = arr[0] = 10. Step 3: arr[0] = arr[0] = 10. Step 4: arr[0] = temp = 10. Result: [10] (unchanged).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Basic skill of looping through array elements and comparing values.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves two core skills: 1) Iterating through all elements of an array using a loop, and 2) Comparing two values to determine which is smaller/larger. The intuition is that to process collections of data, we need to examine each item sequentially, and to make decisions (like finding minimum), we need to compare values. Step-by-step for traversal: initialize index variable, while index < array length: access array[index], process it, increment index. For comparison: use comparison operators (<, >, ==) to compare two values. Time: O(n) for traversal, O(1) for comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This is used in the linear search to find minimum (traverse array, compare each element with current minimum). Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not initializing comparison variable, misunderstanding comparison operators. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): current = arr[i]; # process; if current < min_so_far: min_so_far = current.

examples.input: Traverse array [5, 2, 8, 1] and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0. Step 2: i=0 < len=4, access arr[0]=5, print 5, increment i=1. Step 3: i=1 < 4, access arr[1]=2, print 2, increment i=2. Step 4: i=2 < 4, access arr[2]=8, print 8, increment i=3. Step 5: i=3 < 4, access arr[3]=1, print 1, increment i=4. Step 6: i=4 not < 4, loop ends.

examples.input: Compare values: 3 and 7, determine which is smaller
examples.trace: Step 1: Value A = 3, Value B = 7. Step 2: Apply comparison: 3 < 7 is True. Step 3: Therefore 3 is smaller.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Binary Heap Priority Queue
approaches.summary: Implement a priority queue using a binary heap, where elements are stored in a complete binary tree with heap property, supporting insert and extract-min in logarithmic time. This is a standard efficient method but lacks advanced features like fast decrease-key.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for insert and extract-min, O(1) for find-min

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Priority Queue Using Binary Heap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of a priority queue with binary heap supporting insert, extract-min, find-min, and size operations.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution implementing a priority queue using a binary heap. The intuition is that a binary heap maintains the heap property (min-heap: parent ≤ children) in a complete binary tree structure, allowing efficient priority queue operations. The heap is stored in an array where index relationships represent parent-child connections: for index i, parent at floor((i-1)/2), left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2. Insert adds an element at the end and bubbles it up to maintain heap property. Extract-min removes the root (minimum), replaces it with the last element, and bubbles it down. Find-min returns the root. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize empty list for heap storage. 2) Insert(val): append val, then bubble_up from last index. 3) Extract_min(): if empty return None; store root value; replace root with last element; remove last element; bubble_down from root; return stored value. 4) Find_min(): return root if exists else None. 5) Size(): return length of heap. Time: O(log n) for insert and extract-min (bubble operations traverse tree height), O(1) for find-min and size. Space: O(n) for storing elements. Connection to approach: This implements the complete binary heap priority queue. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty heap in extract-min, incorrect index calculations in bubble operations, not maintaining heap property after operations. Real code pattern: class BinaryHeapPQ with methods __init__, insert, extract_min, find_min, size, _bubble_up, _bubble_down.

examples.input: Initialize pq, insert 5, insert 3, insert 7, extract_min, insert 1, extract_min, find_min
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty heap []. Step 2: Insert 5 -> heap [5]. Step 3: Insert 3 -> append 3 at end [5,3], bubble_up: compare 3 with parent 5 at index 0, swap -> [3,5]. Step 4: Insert 7 -> append 7 [3,5,7], bubble_up: compare 7 with parent 5 at index 1, no swap. Step 5: Extract_min -> root 3 stored, replace root with last element 7 -> [7,5], remove last -> [7,5], bubble_down from root: compare 7 with children 5 (only left child exists), swap -> [5,7]. Return 3. Step 6: Insert 1 -> append 1 [5,7,1], bubble_up: compare 1 with parent 7 at index 1, swap -> [5,1,7]; compare 1 with parent 5 at index 0, swap -> [1,5,7]. Step 7: Extract_min -> root 1 stored, replace with last 7 -> [7,5], remove last -> [7,5], bubble_down: compare 7 with child 5, swap -> [5,7]. Return 1. Step 8: Find_min -> root 5. Output sequence: 3, 1, 5.

examples.input: Initialize pq, insert 10, insert 20, insert 5, insert 15, extract_min, extract_min, insert 2, extract_min, size
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize []. Step 2: Insert 10 -> [10]. Step 3: Insert 20 -> [10,20], bubble_up: no swap. Step 4: Insert 5 -> [10,20,5], bubble_up: compare 5 with parent 10, swap -> [5,20,10]. Step 5: Insert 15 -> [5,20,10,15], bubble_up: compare 15 with parent 20, no swap. Step 6: Extract_min -> store 5, replace with 15 -> [15,20,10], remove last -> [15,20,10], bubble_down: compare 15 with children 20 and 10, swap with smaller child 10 -> [10,20,15]. Return 5. Step 7: Extract_min -> store 10, replace with 15 -> [15,20], remove last -> [15,20], bubble_down: compare 15 with child 20, no swap. Return 10. Step 8: Insert 2 -> [15,20,2], bubble_up: compare 2 with parent 20, swap -> [15,2,20]; compare 2 with parent 15, swap -> [2,15,20]. Step 9: Extract_min -> store 2, replace with 20 -> [20,15], remove last -> [20,15], bubble_down: compare 20 with child 15, swap -> [15,20]. Return 2. Step 10: Size -> 3. Output sequence: 5, 10, 2, 3.

Initialize, insert 5, insert 5, insert 5, extract_min, extract_min, extract_min

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bubble Down (Heapify Down) Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the bubble_down function to restore heap property from a given index after removing or replacing elements.
ladders.explanation: The bubble_down operation is crucial for maintaining the heap property after the root is removed or replaced during extract-min. The intuition is that when a value is placed at a position where it might violate the min-heap property (parent ≤ children), we repeatedly swap it with its smallest child until the property is restored, moving the value down the tree. This ensures the minimum remains at the root. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Start at given index i. 2) While i has at least one child: a) Find smallest child: left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2; compare to find minimum value among children that exist. b) If heap[i] ≤ smallest child, break (heap property satisfied). c) Else, swap heap[i] with smallest child. d) Update i to index of smallest child. Time: O(log n) as it traverses at most the height of the tree. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This is called in extract_min after replacing root with last element. Common mistakes: not checking if children exist before comparing, incorrect index calculations, infinite loop if break condition missing. Real code pattern: def bubble_down(heap, i): n = len(heap); while True: left = 2*i+1; right = 2*i+2; smallest = i; if left < n and heap[left] < heap[smallest]: smallest = left; if right < n and heap[right] < heap[smallest]: smallest = right; if smallest == i: break; swap heap[i], heap[smallest]; i = smallest.

examples.input: Heap [7,5,3,8,6,4,2], bubble_down from index 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Heap [7,5,3,8,6,4,2], i=0, value 7. Step 2: Find children: left index 1 value 5, right index 2 value 3. Smallest child is right (3 < 5). Step 3: Compare 7 > 3, so swap heap[0] and heap[2] -> [3,5,7,8,6,4,2]. Update i=2. Step 4: At i=2, value 7. Children: left index 5 value 4, right index 6 value 2. Smallest child is right (2 < 4). Step 5: Compare 7 > 2, swap heap[2] and heap[6] -> [3,5,2,8,6,4,7]. Update i=6. Step 6: At i=6, value 7. No children (left index 13 out of bounds). Break. Final heap: [3,5,2,8,6,4,7] but note after first swap, smallest became 3 at root, but we continue: actually after step 3, heap [3,5,7,8,6,4,2]; step 4: at i=2 value 7, children 4 and 2, smallest 2, swap -> [3,5,2,8,6,4,7]; i=6 no children. Final: [3,5,2,8,6,4,7]. Correction: initial heap has 2 at leaf, bubble_down should move 7 down: start [7,5,3,8,6,4,2]; i=0, children 5 and 3, smallest 3, swap -> [3,5,7,8,6,4,2]; i=2, children 4 and 2, smallest 2, swap -> [3,5,2,8,6,4,7]; i=6 stop. Output [3,5,2,8,6,4,7].

examples.input: Heap [10,20,30,40,50], bubble_down from index 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Heap [10,20,30,40,50], i=0, value 10. Step 2: Children: left index 1 value 20, right index 2 value 30. Smallest child is left 20. Step 3: Compare 10 ≤ 20, so break. No swaps. Final heap unchanged: [10,20,30,40,50].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Heap Property After Insertion and Extraction
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the bubble-up and bubble-down mechanisms to preserve min-heap structure.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of a binary heap priority queue is maintaining the heap property (min-heap: parent ≤ children) through bubble-up after insertion and bubble-down after extraction. The intuition is that the heap is a complete binary tree where operations temporarily violate the property, and these bubble operations efficiently restore it by moving elements up or down along a single path. Bubble-up is used after inserting a new element at the end: compare with parent, swap if smaller, repeat until root or parent is smaller. Bubble-down is used after extracting the root (removing minimum): replace root with last element, then compare with children, swap with smallest child if larger, repeat until leaf or no violation. These ensure O(log n) time by traversing tree height. Step-by-step: For bubble-up: start at index i, while i > 0 and heap[i] < heap[parent], swap and set i = parent. For bubble-down: as in L1. Time: each O(log n). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Bubble-down is detailed in L1; here we combine both for full maintenance. Common mistakes: off-by-one in parent calculation, not updating index after swap, missing break conditions. Real code pattern: def bubble_up(heap, i): while i > 0: parent = (i-1)//2; if heap[i] >= heap[parent]: break; swap heap[i], heap[parent]; i = parent.

examples.input: Insert 4 into heap [1,3,5,7,9] using bubble-up
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap [1,3,5,7,9]. Step 2: Append 4 -> [1,3,5,7,9,4]. Step 3: Bubble-up from index 5 (value 4). Parent index = (5-1)//2 = 2, value 5. Compare 4 < 5, swap -> [1,3,4,7,9,5]. Update i=2. Step 4: At i=2, value 4. Parent index = (2-1)//2 = 0, value 1. Compare 4 ≥ 1, break. Final heap: [1,3,4,7,9,5].

examples.input: Extract-min from heap [2,5,3,8,6,4] using bubble-down
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap [2,5,3,8,6,4]. Step 2: Extract-min: store root 2. Replace root with last element 4 -> [4,5,3,8,6]. Remove last -> [4,5,3,8,6]. Step 3: Bubble-down from index 0 (value 4). Children: left index 1 value 5, right index 2 value 3. Smallest child is right 3. Compare 4 > 3, swap -> [3,5,4,8,6]. Update i=2. Step 4: At i=2, value 4. Children: left index 5 out of bounds (n=5), no children. Break. Return 2. Final heap: [3,5,4,8,6].

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Representation of Complete Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement index calculations to represent parent-child relationships in a heap stored as an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using an array to represent a complete binary tree, which is essential for efficient heap operations. The intuition is that a complete binary tree can be stored sequentially in an array without pointers, using index arithmetic to navigate parent-child relationships. For a node at index i (0-based): its parent is at floor((i-1)/2), left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2. This compact representation saves space and allows O(1) access. The tree is filled level by level from left to right, ensuring the array corresponds to a complete tree. Step-by-step: 1) Store heap elements in a list. 2) To find parent of index i: if i==0 (root), no parent; else parent_index = (i-1)//2. 3) To find left child: left_index = 2*i+1; check if < len(heap). 4) To find right child: right_index = 2*i+2; check if < len(heap). Time: O(1) for each calculation. Space: O(n) for array. Connection to L2: These calculations are used in bubble-up and bubble-down to traverse the tree. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing incorrectly, not handling root case, off-by-one errors in division. Real code pattern: def parent(i): return (i-1)//2 if i>0 else -1; def left_child(i): return 2*i+1; def right_child(i): return 2*i+2.

examples.input: Array [10,20,30,40,50,60,70], find parent, left child, right child for index 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40, 4:50, 5:60, 6:70. Step 2: For index i=2. Parent: (2-1)//2 = 1//2 = 0. So parent index 0, value 10. Left child: 2*2+1 = 5. Left child index 5 exists (since 5 < 7), value 60. Right child: 2*2+2 = 6. Right child index 6 exists, value 70.

examples.input: Array [5,3,8,1,4], find parent for index 4 and left child for index 1
examples.output: Parent for index 4: 1, Left child for index 1: 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices: 0:5, 1:3, 2:8, 3:1, 4:4. Step 2: For index i=4. Parent: (4-1)//2 = 3//2 = 1. So parent index 1, value 3. Step 3: For index i=1. Left child: 2*1+1 = 3. Left child index 3 exists (3 < 5), value 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Implement a swap function to exchange two elements in an array, used in heap bubble operations.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves swapping two elements in an array, which is fundamental for bubble-up and bubble-down in heaps. The intuition is simple: to exchange values at two indices, use a temporary variable to hold one value during the exchange, preventing data loss. This is a constant-time operation essential for rearranging elements to maintain heap property. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Given array arr and indices i and j. 2) Check if i and j are within bounds (0 ≤ i,j < len(arr)). 3) Store arr[i] in temp variable. 4) Set arr[i] = arr[j]. 5) Set arr[j] = temp. Time: O(1). Space: O(1) for temp variable. Connection to L3: Swapping is used when adjusting positions in the array representation during heap operations. Common mistakes: forgetting to use a temp variable (direct assignment loses data), not checking indices, swapping with self unnecessarily. Real code pattern: def swap(arr, i, j): if 0 <= i < len(arr) and 0 <= j < len(arr): temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp.

examples.input: Array [10,20,30,40], swap indices 1 and 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial array [10,20,30,40]. Step 2: Indices i=1 (value 20), j=3 (value 40). Step 3: temp = arr[1] = 20. Step 4: arr[1] = arr[3] = 40 -> array becomes [10,40,30,40]. Step 5: arr[3] = temp = 20 -> array becomes [10,40,30,20]. Final: [10,40,30,20].

examples.input: Array [5,3,7], swap indices 0 and 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial [5,3,7]. Step 2: i=0 value 5, j=2 value 7. Step 3: temp = 5. Step 4: arr[0] = 7 -> [7,3,7]. Step 5: arr[2] = 5 -> [7,3,5]. Final: [7,3,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn basic array operations: accessing elements by index, appending, and removing elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers fundamental array operations necessary for implementing a heap. An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements accessible by indices starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access to any element by its position, making them efficient for heap storage. Key operations: 1) Access: get element at index i using arr[i]. 2) Append: add element to end using arr.append(val), increasing length by 1. 3) Remove last: remove element from end using arr.pop(), decreasing length by 1 and returning the value. 4) Length: get number of elements using len(arr). These are building blocks for heap operations: insert appends and bubble-up, extract-min removes last and bubble-down. Time: O(1) for access and append/remove last on average (amortized for append). Space: O(n) for storing elements. Connection to L4: Arrays are the data structure where swaps occur. Common mistakes: index out of bounds (e.g., accessing index equal to length), confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, not updating length after operations. Real code pattern: arr = []; arr.append(5); x = arr[0]; last = arr.pop(); n = len(arr).

examples.input: Start with empty array, append 3, append 5, access index 0, remove last, length
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty array []. Step 2: Append 3 -> array becomes [3]. Step 3: Append 5 -> array becomes [3,5]. Step 4: Access index 0 -> value 3. Step 5: Remove last using pop() -> removes 5, array becomes [3], returns 5. Step 6: Length -> len([3]) = 1. Output sequence: after appends array [3,5], access 3, pop returns 5, length 1.

examples.input: Array [10,20,30], access index 2, append 40, remove last, access index 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial array [10,20,30]. Step 2: Access index 2 -> value 30. Step 3: Append 40 -> array becomes [10,20,30,40]. Step 4: Remove last using pop() -> removes 40, array becomes [10,20,30], returns 40. Step 5: Access index 0 -> value 10. Output: access 30, array after append [10,20,30,40], pop returns 40, access 10.

testCases.input: Array [11,12,13,14], remove last, remove last, length
Empty array, append 15, append 16, access index 0, access index 1
Array [25,26], access index 0, append 27, remove last, access index 1

approaches.name: Approach 3: Fibonacci Heap Implementation
approaches.summary: Implement a priority queue using a Fibonacci heap, which maintains a collection of heap-ordered trees with lazy merging and consolidation to achieve amortized constant-time insert and decrease-key, and logarithmic extract-min. This is optimal for algorithms requiring many decrease-key operations.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized for insert and decrease-key, O(log n) amortized for extract-min

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Fibonacci Heap Priority Queue Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement all operations of a priority queue using a Fibonacci heap data structure.
ladders.explanation: A Fibonacci heap is a collection of heap-ordered trees that supports priority queue operations with optimal amortized time complexities. The key insight is lazy merging - we don't immediately consolidate trees after operations, but only when necessary during extract-min. This gives O(1) amortized insert and decrease-key, and O(log n) amortized extract-min. The structure maintains a circular doubly-linked list of tree roots, with each node tracking its degree (number of children), parent pointer, child list, and mark flag for decrease-key optimization. For the full implementation, we need: 1) Insert: Add new node as singleton tree to root list, update min pointer if needed. 2) Extract-min: Remove min node, add its children to root list, then consolidate by merging trees of same degree using an array. 3) Decrease-key: Update key, cut node from parent and add to root list, then perform cascading cuts if parent was marked. 4) Merge: Combine two Fibonacci heaps by concatenating root lists. The amortized analysis uses potential function Φ = trees + 2×marked nodes. Each operation's actual cost plus potential change gives the amortized bounds. Common mistakes include forgetting to update min pointer after operations, incorrect consolidation logic, or mishandling the mark flag during decrease-key. The Fibonacci heap excels in algorithms like Dijkstra's where many decrease-key operations occur.

examples.input: Insert 5, Insert 3, Insert 8, Extract-min, Decrease-key(node with value 8 to 2), Extract-min
examples.trace:
1. Insert 5: Root list = [5], min = 5
2. Insert 3: Root list = [5,3], min = 3 (3 < 5)
3. Insert 8: Root list = [5,3,8], min = 3
4. Extract-min: Remove 3, add its children (none) to root list, root list = [5,8]
   Consolidate: degrees 0 and 0, no same degree trees, min = 5
   Return 3
5. Decrease-key(8→2): Node 8 becomes 2, cut from parent (none since root), add to root list
   Root list = [5,2], min = 2 (2 < 5)
6. Extract-min: Remove 2, add children (none), root list = [5]
   Consolidate: single tree, min = 5
   Return 2

examples.input: Insert 10, Insert 4, Insert 7, Insert 1, Extract-min, Decrease-key(node 10 to 3), Extract-min, Extract-min
examples.output: First extract-min returns 1, second returns 3, third returns 4
examples.trace:
1. Insert 10: Root=[10], min=10
2. Insert 4: Root=[10,4], min=4
3. Insert 7: Root=[10,4,7], min=4
4. Insert 1: Root=[10,4,7,1], min=1
5. Extract-min: Remove 1, children none, root=[10,4,7]
   Consolidate: degrees all 0, no merging needed, min=4
   Return 1
6. Decrease-key(10→3): Node 10→3, cut (root already), root=[4,7,3], min=3
7. Extract-min: Remove 3, children none, root=[4,7]
   Consolidate: degrees 0 and 0, no merge, min=4
   Return 3
8. Extract-min: Remove 4, children none, root=[7], min=7
   Return 4

testCases.input: Insert 7, Insert 7, Insert 7, Extract-min, Extract-min, Extract-min
Insert 8, Insert 4, Insert 12, Extract-min, Decrease-key(node 12 to 2), Extract-min
Insert 6, Insert 2, Insert 9, Insert 1, Extract-min, Extract-min, Extract-min
Insert 15, Insert 3, Insert 8, Extract-min, Insert 1, Extract-min
Insert 5, Insert 10, Decrease-key(node 10 to 3), Extract-min, Extract-min
Insert 20, Insert 15, Insert 25, Extract-min, Decrease-key(node 25 to 5), Extract-min, Extract-min
Insert 30, Insert 10, Insert 20, Extract-min, Insert 5, Extract-min, Extract-min

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Consolidation Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the consolidation process that merges trees of equal degree after extract-min.
ladders.explanation: Consolidation is the critical operation that maintains the Fibonacci heap's efficiency by ensuring no two roots have the same degree. After removing the minimum node during extract-min, we add its children to the root list, which may create multiple trees with the same degree. Consolidation merges these trees pairwise until all roots have unique degrees. The algorithm uses an array A where A[d] points to a tree root with degree d. We iterate through all roots in the root list: for each root x, let d = x.degree. While A[d] is not empty, we have another tree y with same degree d. We link x and y by making the root with larger key a child of the root with smaller key (to maintain heap order), increment degree, and clear A[d]. Then we set A[new degree] = x and continue. After processing all roots, A contains the new root list with unique degrees. The intuition is that by keeping degrees unique, we ensure the number of trees is O(log n), which gives the O(log n) amortized time for extract-min. The linking always preserves heap order (parent ≤ children). Time complexity is O(number of roots + max degree) which amortizes to O(log n). Space is O(log n) for the array. Common mistakes include not updating child/parent pointers correctly during linking, or forgetting to clear array positions after merging.

examples.input: Root list with trees of degrees: 0, 0, 1, 2, 0
examples.output: Consolidated root list with unique degrees
examples.trace:
Initial roots: A(0), B(0), C(1), D(2), E(0)
Process A(0): A[0]=A
Process B(0): A[0] occupied by A, link B to A (A smaller), A.degree=1, clear A[0], A[1]=A
Process C(1): A[1] occupied by A, link C to A (A smaller), A.degree=2, clear A[1], A[2]=A
Process D(2): A[2] occupied by A, link D to A (A smaller), A.degree=3, clear A[2], A[3]=A
Process E(0): A[0] empty, A[0]=E
Result: Roots A(3) and E(0), degrees unique

examples.input: Root list with degrees: 1, 1, 2
examples.trace:
Roots: X(1), Y(1), Z(2)
Process X(1): A[1]=X
Process Y(1): A[1] occupied by X, link Y to X (assume X smaller), X.degree=2, clear A[1], A[2]=X
Process Z(2): A[2] occupied by X, link Z to X (X smaller), X.degree=3, clear A[2], A[3]=X
Result: Single root X with degree 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Lazy Merging with Delayed Consolidation
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the lazy approach where trees are only consolidated during extract-min.
ladders.explanation: The core innovation of Fibonacci heaps is lazy merging: instead of immediately merging trees after each insert (like binomial heaps), we postpone consolidation until absolutely necessary during extract-min. When we insert a new element, we simply add it as a singleton tree to the root list in O(1) time. Multiple inserts create many trees in the root list, but we don't merge them yet. This gives fast inserts. Only when we need to find the new minimum after extract-min do we consolidate all trees to ensure no two have the same degree. The intuition is that most operations don't require the heap to be fully consolidated - we only need the minimum element accessible (which we track with a min pointer). The potential function in amortized analysis counts the number of trees, so inserts increase potential slightly, while extract-min's consolidation reduces it significantly, balancing the costs. This lazy approach is why decrease-key can also be O(1) - we just cut nodes and add them to the root list without immediate consolidation. The trade-off is that extract-min becomes more expensive O(log n), but this is optimal when there are many inserts/decrease-keys and few extract-mins. Implementation requires maintaining a circular doubly-linked list of roots for O(1) concatenation, and a min pointer that's updated on inserts and decrease-keys. Common mistakes include consolidating too early or forgetting to update the min pointer after lazy operations.

examples.input: Insert 5, Insert 3, Insert 8 (lazy, no consolidation)
examples.trace:
1. Insert 5: Create tree with single node 5, add to root list, min=5
2. Insert 3: Create tree with single node 3, add to root list, min=3 (update since 3<5)
3. Insert 8: Create tree with single node 8, add to root list, min remains 3
Root list has 3 trees: [5,3,8], no merging done yet

examples.input: After several inserts, root list has trees of various degrees, then extract-min triggers consolidation
examples.output: Consolidation merges trees of same degree
examples.trace:
Suppose root list has trees with degrees: A(0), B(2), C(0), D(1), E(2)
Extract-min called:
1. Remove min node
2. Add its children to root list (none in this example)
3. Consolidate: Process all roots
   - Merge trees of same degree: B(2) and E(2) merge into one tree degree 3
   - Others remain
Result: Fewer trees with unique degrees

testCases.input: Insert 100, lazy, then check root count
Multiple inserts creating 5 trees, then lazy state
Insert 8, Insert 4, lazy, root list size
Insert 15, Insert 10, Insert 5, min after lazy inserts
Insert 3, Insert 7, Insert 2, lazy, trees degrees

ladders.title: Building Block — Heap-Ordered Multi-tree Structure
ladders.desc: Implement and manage a collection of heap-ordered trees with parent-child relationships.
ladders.explanation: A Fibonacci heap is not a single tree but a collection of heap-ordered trees, where each tree satisfies the heap property: parent key ≤ children keys. Each node has: key value, parent pointer, circular doubly-linked list of children, left/right pointers for sibling connections, degree (number of children), and mark flag. The roots of all trees are connected in a circular doubly-linked list. This structure allows efficient merging (just concatenate lists) and child management. The heap property ensures the minimum key is always at a root (specifically the root pointed by min pointer). When we link two trees during consolidation, we make the root with larger key a child of the root with smaller key, preserving heap order. Children are added to the child list in O(1) using circular linking. The degree tracks how many children a node has, crucial for consolidation. The mark flag indicates whether a node has lost a child since becoming a child itself (used in decrease-key cascading cuts). Managing this structure requires careful pointer updates when adding/removing nodes. Time complexity for basic tree operations is O(1). Space is O(1) per node. Common mistakes include breaking circular links, not updating parent pointers when moving children, or incorrect degree updates.

examples.input: Create two heap-ordered trees: Tree1 root=5 with child 7, Tree2 root=3 with children 4 and 6
examples.trace:
Tree1: Root node 5, degree=1, child list contains node 7 (parent=5)
Tree2: Root node 3, degree=2, child list contains nodes 4 and 6 (both parent=3)
Heap property: 5≤7, 3≤4, 3≤6
Root list: circular link between 5 and 3
Min pointer: points to 3 (smallest root)

examples.input: Link Tree1 (root=5) and Tree2 (root=3) during consolidation
examples.trace:
Compare roots: 3 < 5, so make 5 a child of 3
1. Remove 5 from root list
2. Add 5 to child list of 3 (circular link)
3. Set 5.parent = 3
4. Increment 3.degree from 2 to 3
5. Update child list: 3's children now 4,6,5
Heap property maintained: 3≤5

testCases.input: Two trees roots 2 and 7, check heap order
Tree root=3 with child 5, add new child 4
Remove child from parent, update degree

testCases.expected: Each root's left/right point to other

testCases.input: Node with parent and children, check pointers

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Circular Doubly-Linked List Management
ladders.desc: Implement insertion, deletion, and concatenation in a circular doubly-linked list.
ladders.explanation: Fibonacci heaps use circular doubly-linked lists to connect sibling nodes (roots or children). Each node has left and right pointers. In a circular list, the last node's right points to first, and first's left points to last. This allows O(1) insertion/deletion anywhere and O(1) concatenation of two lists. To insert node x after node y: x.left = y, x.right = y.right, y.right.left = x, y.right = x. To delete node x: x.left.right = x.right, x.right.left = x.left. To concatenate two lists L1 and L2: if both non-empty, connect end of L1 to start of L2 and vice versa via pointer updates. An empty list is represented by null. The circular structure eliminates special cases for head/tail. For Fibonacci heaps, we maintain a root list (circular) and each node's child list (circular). When adding a new singleton tree, we insert it into the root list. When removing a node (e.g., during extract-min), we delete it from its list. Concatenation is used when adding a node's children to the root list. Time complexity is O(1) for all operations. Space is O(1) extra pointers. Common mistakes include forgetting to update both left and right pointers, mishandling empty lists, or creating broken links.

examples.input: List with nodes [A], insert B after A
examples.trace:
Initial: A.left=A, A.right=A (self-loop)
Insert B after A:
B.left = A
B.right = A.right = A
A.right.left = B (A.left = B)
A.right = B
Now: A.right=B, B.left=A, B.right=A, A.left=B

examples.input: List [X,Y,Z] circular, delete Y
examples.trace:
Before: X.right=Y, Y.left=X, Y.right=Z, Z.left=Y, Z.right=X, X.left=Z
Delete Y:
Y.left.right = Y.right → X.right = Z
Y.right.left = Y.left → Z.left = X
Now: X.right=Z, Z.left=X, Z.right=X, X.left=Z

testCases.input: Circular list traversal from any node
Insert after last node in circular list

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Priority Queue with Heap Property
ladders.desc: Understand and implement basic priority queue operations maintaining heap order.
ladders.explanation: A priority queue is a data structure that supports insert (add element with priority) and extract-min (remove and return element with smallest priority). The heap property ensures that in a tree structure, each parent's priority is less than or equal to its children's priorities (min-heap). This guarantees the minimum element is at the root. For a basic implementation, we can use a single binary heap tree. Insert: Add new element at bottom, then bubble up by swapping with parent until heap property restored. Extract-min: Remove root, replace with last element, then bubble down by swapping with smaller child until heap property restored. The intuition is that maintaining partial order (heap property) is sufficient to quickly access the minimum, without needing full sorting. Time complexity is O(log n) for both operations in a binary heap. Space is O(n). This foundation is essential because Fibonacci heap extends this with multiple trees and lazy operations for better amortized times. Common mistakes include incorrect bubble-up/down logic, off-by-one errors in array indices (if using array representation), or not handling empty queue cases.

examples.input: Insert 5, Insert 3, Insert 8 into empty priority queue
examples.trace:
Start empty
Insert 5: [5] (root)
Insert 3: Add at end [5,3], bubble up: 3<5 so swap → [3,5]
Insert 8: Add at end [3,5,8], bubble up: 8≥5 so stop
Final heap: root=3, children 5 and 8

examples.input: Extract-min from heap [3,5,8,10,7]
examples.trace:
Heap: 3
     / \
    5   8
   / \
  10  7
Extract-min: Remove 3, replace with last element 7 → [7,5,8,10]
Bubble down: Compare 7 with children 5 and 8, 5 smaller, swap 7 and 5 → [5,7,8,10]
Now 7 has children 10 (only left child), 7≤10 so stop
New heap: root=5, children 7 and 8, 7 has child 10

testCases.input: Insert 6, Insert 2, Insert 5, Extract-min, Insert 1, Extract-min
Multiple inserts in descending order, extract-min
